purpose_capability: The code and install (npm @wipcomputer/wip-release) match the stated purpose: bumping package.json, updating SKILL.md and CHANGELOG.md, committing/tagging, and publishing to npm/GitHub/ClawHub. Requiring git, npm, gh, and op is appropriate for these operations.
instruction_scope: SKILL.md and core.mjs include behaviors beyond a simple version bump: (1) fetching npm token via 1Password (expected for publish), (2) optional copying SKILL.md into a website repo and running deploy.sh (this will execute arbitrary code in another repo if configured), and (3) strict gating that requires release notes to come from a file on disk. The MCP server wrapper forces notes to be passed as a flag (notesSource='flag'), which conflicts with the tool's own rule that notes must be file-backed and may cause releases to block — a functional inconsistency.
install_mechanism: Install uses a published npm package (@wipcomputer/wip-release) which matches package.json that creates a 'wip-release' binary. No downloads from unknown hosts or suspicious install mechanics are present.
credentials: The skill expects access to credential material needed for publishing: 1Password service account token path (~/.openclaw/secrets/op-sa-token) and an npm token stored in a vault. Those are proportionate to publishing but are sensitive. SKILL.md mentions WIP_WEBSITE_REPO (optional) used to run deploy.sh; that grants the skill the ability to operate on another repository if configured. The registry metadata shows no required env vars, which is inconsistent with SKILL.md's declared secret requirements.
persistence_privilege: always:false and the skill does not request system-wide persistence or modifications of other skills' configs. It exposes an MCP server to let agents call its tools; autonomous invocation is allowed by default (platform normal) but combined with credential access is something to be aware of.
assessment: This tool mostly does what it claims, but review these points before installing: - Credentials: It uses the 1Password CLI (op) and expects a service-account token at ~/.openclaw/secrets/op-sa-token and an 'npm Token' in an 'Agent Secrets' vault to perform npm publishes. Only install if you trust the code and are comfortable granting it access to those secrets. - Website publishing: If you configure a website repo (via .publish-skill.json or WIP_WEBSITE_REPO), the tool will copy SKILL.md into that repo and run deploy.sh — that means it can execute scripts in the target repo. Only set that variable for a repo you control and have audited. - MCP / automation discrepancy: The MCP server wrapper marks the 'notes' argument as required and passes notesSource='flag', but the release pipeline enforces that release notes must be a committed file on disk. This mismatch may cause the MCP-based tool to fail for blocked releases (minor/major). If you intend to call this via agents/MCP, test the behavior carefully. - Code review suggestions: Inspect getNpmToken() (used by publishNpm) to confirm how it reads 1Password, and audit any places that call external scripts or move files (e.g., running deploy.sh, renaming RELEASE-NOTES files). Confirm that usage of child_process (execSync/execFileSync) is limited to expected CLI calls (git, npm, gh, clawhub) and that user-supplied strings are passed as argv arrays (the project claims to use execFileSync to mitigate shell injection). - Operational advice: Consider forking and running locally (or running in a sandboxed CI job) until you've verified behavior, particularly publish steps and website deploy. If you will expose the MCP interface to agents, restrict which agents can call it and ensure they should have access to publishing credentials. Static analysis: 3 patterns detected Details ▾

purpose_capability: Name/description match the implementation: the code uses the GitHub CLI (gh) to check repo metadata and enforces a -private counterpart rule. Required binaries (node, gh) are appropriate and expected for this functionality.
instruction_scope: SKILL.md and guard.mjs only direct the agent to inspect Bash commands (via PreToolUse) and call the local CLI/module functions. The runtime instructions do not ask the agent to read unrelated files or exfiltrate data. The hook reads JSON on stdin and returns allow/deny decisions only.
install_mechanism: Install is via an npm package (@wipcomputer/wip-repo-permissions-hook), a standard package source. There are no arbitrary URL downloads or extract-from-unknown-host steps in the manifest.
credentials: The skill does not declare environment variables but depends on the gh CLI being present and authenticated. That means GitHub credentials (via gh auth or GH_TOKEN or user config) are required in practice; the package itself does not request or store extra unrelated secrets.
persistence_privilege: The skill is not marked always:true and does not attempt to modify other skills or global agent config beyond being installed as an extension/hook (the user explicitly copies/symlinks it). Its MCP server exposes tools over stdio, which is consistent with the declared MCP integration.
assessment: This package appears to do what it claims: it runs local gh CLI commands to decide whether to block making a repo public. Before installing, check these things: (1) The npm package and its GitHub repo (https://github.com/wipcomputer/wip-ai-devops-toolbox) to confirm provenance and review change history. (2) Ensure your gh CLI is configured with an account or token that has only the minimum GitHub scopes needed; the tool will call GitHub APIs on your behalf. (3) Installing the npm package will place code on disk and allow it to run as a hook (PreToolUse or an OpenClaw extension); review file install locations and permission changes. (4) If you expose the MCP server to other agents/processes, consider limiting which agents can call it since it can audit and check org repos. Finally, this hook enforces a local policy but can be bypassed if users call GitHub directly or use other tools — treat it as a helpful guard, not an absolute enforcement mechanism. purpose_capability: The name/description, install spec, and code all align: this is an installer that detects interfaces and installs them to user-scoped agent runtimes. Requiring node/npm/git is appropriate. However, the installer intentionally modifies user agent config directories (~/.ldm, ~/.openclaw, ~/.claude) and registers MCP servers — behaviors that are consistent with an installer but are high-impact and should be noted as beyond a simple 'npm install' operation.
instruction_scope: SKILL.md shows normal usage, but the runtime code (install.js) performs many side effects: rm -rf on old extension directories, copy files into ~/.openclaw/extensions and ~/.ldm/extensions, write/update registry.json, and modify ~/.claude settings/.mcp.json. The code also invokes external commands like `claude mcp add/remove` (the 'claude' CLI) which is not declared in requires.bins. These are broad, potentially destructive operations and are not fully surfaced in the top-level requires/manifest.
install_mechanism: The npm package install route (@wipcomputer/universal-installer) is standard and low risk for fetching this skill itself. However, the installer executes `npm install -g .` (and `npm link`) on target repos it installs, which will run those repos' install/build scripts as the invoking user. That means installing an arbitrary repo via this tool executes untrusted code from that repo — expected for an installer but a meaningful execution risk.
credentials: The skill declares no required env vars, which is correct, but it reads HOME and writes/edits several user-level config files (OpenClaw, Claude, LDM). It can modify agent settings that may contain credentials or control agent behavior. Also the code calls the `claude` CLI even though 'claude' is not listed in required binaries — an incoherence that could cause silent failures or unexpected side effects when present.
persistence_privilege: The skill is not forced-always, but it asks to install and persist tools into user-level extension directories and to maintain a registry (~/.ldm/extensions/registry.json). It also migrates/removes existing installs (rm -rf) and edits other agent configs. Modifying other agents' configuration and removing directories on the filesystem are privileged, high-impact actions for a user-level installer and should be treated cautiously.
assessment: This tool is an installer and will make wide-reaching changes in your home directory: copying files into ~/.ldm and ~/.openclaw, updating ~/.claude settings, registering/removing MCP servers, and removing old extension directories (rm -rf). It also runs `npm install -g .` on repos you ask it to install — which executes those repos' install/build scripts (arbitrary code as your user). Before running or installing this skill, consider: 1) only use it with repositories you trust; 2) run with --dry-run first to see what it will detect; 3) inspect the install.js and target repo install scripts manually; 4) backup or snapshot (~/.claude, ~/.openclaw, ~/.ldm) before use; 5) prefer running in an isolated account/container if you want to limit blast radius; and 6) note the code invokes the 'claude' CLI even though it's not declared — ensure you understand what that CLI does on your system. If you need the installer but worry about side effects, consider forking and removing automatic destructive operations (migration/removal) and interactiveizing confirmations. Static analysis: 1 pattern detected Details ▾

sections.Purpose & Capability: The name/description, install spec, and code all align: this is an installer that detects interfaces and installs them to user-scoped agent runtimes. Requiring node/npm/git is appropriate. However, the installer intentionally modifies user agent config directories (~/.ldm, ~/.openclaw, ~/.claude) and registers MCP servers — behaviors that are consistent with an installer but are high-impact and should be noted as beyond a simple 'npm install' operation.
sections.Instruction Scope: SKILL.md shows normal usage, but the runtime code (install.js) performs many side effects: rm -rf on old extension directories, copy files into ~/.openclaw/extensions and ~/.ldm/extensions, write/update registry.json, and modify ~/.claude settings/.mcp.json. The code also invokes external commands like `claude mcp add/remove` (the 'claude' CLI) which is not declared in requires.bins. These are broad, potentially destructive operations and are not fully surfaced in the top-level requires/manifest.
sections.Install Mechanism: The npm package install route (@wipcomputer/universal-installer) is standard and low risk for fetching this skill itself. However, the installer executes `npm install -g .` (and `npm link`) on target repos it installs, which will run those repos' install/build scripts as the invoking user. That means installing an arbitrary repo via this tool executes untrusted code from that repo — expected for an installer but a meaningful execution risk.
sections.Credentials: The skill declares no required env vars, which is correct, but it reads HOME and writes/edits several user-level config files (OpenClaw, Claude, LDM). It can modify agent settings that may contain credentials or control agent behavior. Also the code calls the `claude` CLI even though 'claude' is not listed in required binaries — an incoherence that could cause silent failures or unexpected side effects when present.
sections.Persistence & Privilege: The skill is not forced-always, but it asks to install and persist tools into user-level extension directories and to maintain a registry (~/.ldm/extensions/registry.json). It also migrates/removes existing installs (rm -rf) and edits other agent configs. Modifying other agents' configuration and removing directories on the filesystem are privileged, high-impact actions for a user-level installer and should be treated cautiously.
sections.Assessment: This tool is an installer and will make wide-reaching changes in your home directory: copying files into ~/.ldm and ~/.openclaw, updating ~/.claude settings, registering/removing MCP servers, and removing old extension directories (rm -rf). It also runs `npm install -g .` on repos you ask it to install — which executes those repos' install/build scripts (arbitrary code as your user). Before running or installing this skill, consider: 1) only use it with repositories you trust; 2) run with --dry-run first to see what it will detect; 3) inspect the install.js and target repo install scripts manually; 4) backup or snapshot (~/.claude, ~/.openclaw, ~/.ldm) before use; 5) prefer running in an isolated account/container if you want to limit blast radius; and 6) note the code invokes the 'claude' CLI even though it's not declared — ensure you understand what that CLI does on your system. If you need the installer but worry about side effects, consider forking and removing automatic destructive operations (migration/removal) and interactiveizing confirmations. Static analysis: 1 pattern detected Details ▾

purpose_capability: Name/description, required binary (node), and bundled Docsify assets match an S3-based markdown-sharing tool. Requesting a config.json with S3 access_key/secret_key and public_url is coherent for uploading and serving .md files.
instruction_scope: SKILL.md gives narrowly scoped runtime instructions: check/write {SKILL_DIR}/config.json, create a temp .md, and run `node {SKILL_DIR}/upload.js <local-file> <remote-key>`. It explicitly warns against auto-triggering and states uploads are only to the user's configured S3 endpoint. This is appropriate, but the agent will write/retain plaintext credentials in config.json (the doc says gitignored) — users should be aware of that storage of secrets.
install_mechanism: No install spec is present (instruction-only install), and all Docsify assets are bundled locally. That is low-risk. However, an executable script (upload.js) will be run via node; its contents were not available in the provided excerpt, preventing verification of its behavior.
credentials: Only a per-bucket S3 credential/config (access_key, secret_key, endpoint, bucket, public_url, optional region/expire_days) is requested — proportionate to the stated purpose. Note: storing credentials plaintext in config.json and recommending Admin Read & Write for lifecycle-rule automation increases privilege scope; users should prefer least-privilege tokens scoped to the specific bucket.
persistence_privilege: The skill is not always-enabled and does not request system-wide privileges. It writes its own config.json in {SKILL_DIR}, which is normal for a skill. No evidence it modifies other skills or system settings.
assessment: This skill appears coherent with its stated purpose: it bundles a Docsify site and a Node uploader that writes your .md to a user-configured S3-compatible bucket and returns a public URL. Important points before installing: - I could not review the full upload.js contents (the provided file listing included it, but its source was not present in the excerpt). Because that script will be executed with your credentials, you should inspect upload.js yourself to confirm it only talks to the configured S3 endpoint and does not send data elsewhere. - Uploaded files are publicly accessible. Don’t upload secrets or private data. Use the public_url/custom domain you trust. - Provide the least-privilege S3 token possible (restrict to the specific bucket; avoid account-wide keys). If the script's lifecycle rule automation needs Admin privileges, consider granting only object read/write and setting lifecycle rules manually in the provider dashboard. - The skill stores access_key/secret_key in plaintext at {SKILL_DIR}/config.json; ensure filesystem permissions are restricted and the file is excluded from any repository. - Confirm the skill does not auto-trigger uploads (SKILL.md instructs asking the user first). If you lack the ability to review upload.js, treat the skill as higher risk and avoid installing or provide only temporary/limited credentials. Static analysis: 1 pattern detected Details ▾

purpose_capability: The repository and SKILL.md describe a branding agent that scaffolds projects, enforces frameworks (RACE) and provides templates — which matches the included docs and templates. However there are mismatches: SKILL.md/installation says 'Ensure you have Python 3 installed' while README/package.json and the CLI are Node.js-based. Development docs reference scripts (e.g., scripts/setup_branding_project.py) and automation flows that are not present in the file list. These inconsistencies suggest sloppy packaging or incomplete artifacts, not that the required capabilities are malicious, but they are disproportionate to a polished release.
instruction_scope: Runtime instructions are reasonable for the stated purpose: they create a clients/ directory, ask you to drop briefings into client_intel, and the agent analyzes those documents. That behavior is expected for a branding tool. Caveats: the SKILL.md/README assert the agent will 'analyze' and 'run' automated workflows but there is no bundled code for AI integration (no connector to Claude/other LLMs) — the agent is expected to do that. Also the repo includes extensive extracted prompts and OCR text (potentially sensitive/copyrighted content) which will be copied into projects if templates are used.
install_mechanism: There is no install spec in the skill registry (instruction-only). The package contains a small CLI (bin/cli.js) and a package.json so it can be used via npm/npx. No external downloads or archive extraction are present. Overall install risk is low, but the README suggests publishing to npm and automations that would require adding secrets (docs/SETUP_AUTOMATION.md), so users should avoid blindly following those steps without review.
credentials: The skill declares no required environment variables or credentials, which is appropriate. However docs reference an automation token (CLAWDHUB_TOKEN) for publishing and suggest adding GitHub secrets, and README mentions npm publish — these are maintainer-side steps, not runtime requirements. This mismatch (no required env but docs instruct setting secrets for automation) should be treated as a packaging/documentation inconsistency that could confuse less-technical users.
persistence_privilege: The skill does not request persistent or elevated privileges (always:false). The CLI creates folders and copies templates into the current working directory; it does not modify other skills or global agent configuration. That scope is proportional to a scaffolding tool.
assessment: Before installing or running this skill, consider the following: - Source verification: The skill's registry entry has no homepage and an unknown source. Treat it as untrusted until you can verify the upstream repository or author. - Review mismatches: SKILL.md asks for Python but the repo is Node-based (package.json, bin/cli.js). README mentions scripts that are absent. These indicate the package may be incomplete or incorrectly documented — inspect files before running. - Inspect code before execution: The CLI is small and appears to only create directories and copy templates, but review bin/cli.js (and any other scripts) to ensure no network calls or unexpected exec/IO happen. Note: the CLI imports child_process.execSync though it isn't used — confirm no hidden exec usage. - Run in a safe directory: Execute the CLI in an empty or disposable folder (not your home or a workspace with secrets) to avoid accidental copying or overwriting. - Protect client data: The skill asks you to drop client briefings, transcripts, and PDFs into client_intel. Only upload data you are permitted to share; do not place PII or confidential documents until you confirm where (and how) the agent sends/uses that data. - Check templates/content: The repository contains extracted prompts and OCRed text that may include copyrighted or sensitive content. Decide if you want those artifacts copied into client projects. - Avoid publishing secrets: The docs describe adding CLAWDHUB_TOKEN to GitHub secrets to automate publishing. Do not add secrets or publish the repo unless you fully control the account and understand the automation steps. If you want a safer path: ask the maintainer for an authoritative source (official repo URL), request a minimal reproducible release (only the templates and CLI with clear README), or run the tool in an isolated environment / sandbox and review its actions before using with real client data.

purpose_capability: Name/description match the included files: a CLI, local UI, and API reference for LightningEX. Network calls target api.lightningex.io and the CLI serves/requests data consistent with an exchange client. No unrelated binaries, env vars, or config paths are requested.
instruction_scope: SKILL.md tells users to run the included CLI and launch a local web UI; the repo contains a CLI that issues HTTPS requests to api.lightningex.io and an HTML UI that (per references) generates a client fingerprint from browser characteristics and sends it as a payload. This fingerprinting is expected for the described risk-assessment flow but is a privacy concern. The UI also loads external resources (Google Fonts, a QR code CDN) which will cause the host to contact third-party CDNs from the user's machine.
install_mechanism: There is no install spec in the registry (instruction-only). The package.json and scripts are included so users can run the CLI locally; nothing in the skill attempts to download arbitrary remote archives or run external installers. Note: running the provided CLI requires executing the bundled script (it is executable via its shebang or via `node scripts/cli.js`).
credentials: The skill declares no required environment variables or credentials and the code references only a simple NO_COLOR env var. It does, however, collect a client fingerprint in the UI (userAgent, screen, canvas/WebGL, etc.) which is used as a 'payload' for order placement—this is functionally justified by the API docs but is additional privacy-sensitive data.
persistence_privilege: The skill is not always-enabled and does not request elevated/persistent privileges. It does not declare actions that would modify other agent settings or persist credentials. Autonomous invocation is allowed by default but not a specific risk here.
assessment: This package appears to be a legitimate LightningEX client (CLI + local UI). Before running it: 1) Verify the source—there's no homepage or upstream repository listed; treat it as untrusted code. 2) Inspect scripts/cli.js and the UI JavaScript for any unexpected network endpoints or file I/O you don't want to run. 3) Be aware the web UI generates a browser fingerprint (userAgent, screen, canvas/WebGL, etc.) and sends it to the exchange for risk assessment—this is privacy-sensitive. 4) The UI loads external CDNs (fonts, QR script); your machine will contact those hosts. 5) Never paste or store private keys or seed phrases in the UI/CLI; only provide public receive addresses. 6) If you want extra safety, run the CLI/UI in an isolated environment (container, VM) and monitor outbound network traffic to confirm only api.lightningex.io and expected CDNs are contacted. If you cannot verify the upstream source, treat the package as untrusted.

purpose_capability: Name/description (system security audit, local reports, optional remote enrichment) align with the files and behavior: SKILL.md documents local-only and '--push' modes, the included script performs system queries and generates local reports, and the network endpoints in the doc match the described Changeway push. The skill does not request unrelated cloud credentials or unrelated system-wide access.
instruction_scope: Instructions explicitly read/write files under ~/.openclaw, create a persistent agent_id, optionally read the local Skill list, and run the included Node.js script; all of these are within the stated audit purpose. The push mode will upload MAC address, hostname, agent_id and the local Skill list — this is sensitive data but is documented and gated behind an explicit consent flow. The SKILL.md forbids including full script output when reporting to the user, and requires cron tasks to avoid '--push'.
install_mechanism: No external download/install spec; the skill ships the audit script in the bundle and uses only Node.js built-ins and spawnSync for local commands. No archived artifacts are fetched from untrusted URLs. This is a low-risk install mechanism; the main runtime requirement is Node.js v18+.
credentials: The skill requests no environment variables or cloud credentials. It will read system state (host name, network interfaces/MACs, installed Skill list) and write files under ~/.openclaw (reports, agent_id). Those accesses are relevant to an audit but are privacy-sensitive. The SKILL.md mentions openclaw CLI as an optional/used tool — the registry metadata lists no required binaries, which is a small mismatch (Node.js and optional openclaw CLI are required/used at runtime).
persistence_privilege: The skill persists an agent_id and report files under ~/.openclaw and instructs how to add an OpenClaw cron job (openclaw cron add). always:false and normal autonomous invocation are used. Persisting an agent_id and adding cron tasks are reasonable for an audit tool, but the persistent agent_id plus the ability to upload device identifiers (if user later consents) increases privacy implications — SKILL.md documents and limits this, forbidding cron from using '--push'.
assessment: What to consider before installing: - Functionality and privacy: The tool runs local system checks and stores reports under ~/.openclaw/. It can optionally upload MAC address, hostname, a persistent agent_id and a full local Skill list to https://auth.ctct.cn when you explicitly choose '--push'. That upload is sensitive; only enable it if you trust the Changeway endpoint and the skill author. - Consent gating: The SKILL.md requires an explicit '2 已了解' confirmation before performing '--push' and forbids scheduling '--push' in cron. Do not bypass that manual consent requirement. - Dependencies & runtime: You need Node.js v18+ to run the script. The script also calls the openclaw CLI for some checks (optional but used); if openclaw is missing those checks will be skipped. The registry metadata does not list these binaries—verify you have Node.js and (if you want full checks) openclaw CLI installed. - Persistence: The script creates ~/.openclaw/.agent-id and report files. If you prefer no persistent identifiers, avoid running '--push' and consider deleting ~/.openclaw after testing. - Safety review: The shipped script uses spawnSync with a hardcoded command whitelist (reducing injection risk) and does not download code at runtime. Still, review the script yourself or run it in a disposable/isolated environment (VM or container) first to confirm behavior and that endpoints are acceptable. - Cron and automation: The skill strongly recommends using 'openclaw cron add' and explicitly forbids adding '--push' to scheduled jobs. If you add a scheduled job, verify the cron's message/command does not include '--push' and that the session/isolation settings meet your security policy. - If unsure: Run the script in local-only mode first (no network) to inspect the generated report and ensure it behaves as you expect before consenting to any remote upload or scheduling. If you want, I can point out exact lines in the provided script that collect device identifiers and build the upload payload so you can inspect them more closely. Static analysis: 1 pattern detected Details ▾

assessment: This skill appears to do what it claims: it needs your OpenRouter API key and will read and update ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json to set primary and fallback models. Before installing: (1) back up ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json, (2) review the included Python files yourself (they are small and only call openrouter.ai), (3) be aware the watcher can run continuously and will make network calls using your OPENROUTER_API_KEY, so keep that key private, and (4) note the minor metadata mismatch (registry summary omitted the required env var) — prefer installing from a trusted marketplace or the author's verified repository. purpose_capability: The skill claims to trade Polymarket fast markets using Simmer API and CEX signals; the code calls Polymarket/CLOB endpoints and loads a simmer-sdk client, which is coherent. However, registry metadata at the top declared no required env vars/credentials while clawhub.json and SKILL.md require SIMMER_API_KEY (declared in clawhub.json) and the SKILL.md also instructs the user to provide a WALLET_PRIVATE_KEY — that private key is not declared in the registry metadata or clawhub.json, creating an inconsistency.
instruction_scope: SKILL.md explicitly instructs obtaining SIMMER_API_KEY and a wallet private key (WALLET_PRIVATE_KEY) and storing them as env vars for automated live trading; these steps are within the skill's trading purpose but introduce sensitive handling of secrets. The instructions also recommend running cron/heartbeats and modifying system scheduling — expected for an automated trading skill but high-risk if run on shared systems.
install_mechanism: There is no explicit install spec in the registry entry (instruction-only), which is lower-risk, but clawhub.json declares a pip dependency on 'simmer-sdk'. That means the runtime will require installing a third-party pip package; this is expected for a Simmer-integrated trader but you should verify the package (name, source, and integrity) before installing.
credentials: The skill requires sensitive credentials: SIMMER_API_KEY (declared) and — per SKILL.md — a WALLET_PRIVATE_KEY for live trading. Requesting a wallet private key is proportionate to fully automated trading, but storing a private key in an environment variable is risky (process leaks, logs, backups). Moreover, the WALLET_PRIVATE_KEY is not consistently declared in metadata, which makes it easy for users to miss how sensitive the setup is.
persistence_privilege: The skill is not marked 'always:true' and is user-invocable only; it writes local state (daily_spend.json) in its own directory and will be scheduled by the user. It does not request system-wide privileges or attempt to modify other skills. Autonomous invocation is allowed (platform default) but not escalated here.
assessment: This skill is coherent with its stated function (automated Polymarket sprint trading), but it asks you to provide highly sensitive secrets and has mismatched metadata. Before installing: (1) Verify the skill’s source and the simmer-sdk package (PyPI name and maintainer) — install only from trusted sources. (2) Do not reuse a main wallet private key — create a dedicated low-balance wallet for automated trades. Consider using an external signer/HW wallet or ephemeral signing mechanism instead of putting a private key in an environment variable. (3) Run the script in dry-run mode first and inspect network calls and logs. (4) Review and possibly limit daily_budget and max_position envs to small amounts. (5) Confirm SIMMER_API_KEY scope and revoke it if you stop using the skill. (6) Note the registry mismatch: WALLET_PRIVATE_KEY is required by SKILL.md but not declared in the top-level metadata — ask the publisher to correct the manifest before trusting it.

purpose_capability: Name, description, reference personas, report template, verdict rules, and the price-fetching script all match the stated goal of producing a committee investment report. The included fetch_price.py calls stooq.com (expected for price data) and the references define each committee member's analysis framework — these are proportionate to the skill's purpose.
instruction_scope: SKILL.md instructs the agent to: (1) spawn five independent sub-agents in parallel, (2) use web_search to supplement fundamentals, (3) aggregate results and 'send to current Discord channel', and (4) write archived reports to workspace history. Spawning parallel sessions and reading local reference files is consistent with the skill, but 'send to current Discord channel' references an external endpoint/communication channel that is not declared or explained (no webhook/token or explicit integration). The skill also directs use of web_search (normal) but does not constrain what user data may be included in outbound queries. The references instruct the agent to impersonate named public figures (personas) — this is functional for analysis but may raise policy or trust considerations.
install_mechanism: No install spec; instruction-only plus a small local Python script. No downloads or third-party installs. Low install risk.
credentials: Declared requirements list no environment variables or credentials, yet the runtime flow requires sending results to 'current Discord channel' (an external destination) and saving files under {workspace}. There is no declaration of a Discord webhook, token, or other credential; that gap is an incoherence. The skill otherwise does not request unrelated secrets and the fetch_price.py uses only stooq.com (no API key).
persistence_privilege: always is false; the skill has no elevated platform privileges. It writes archives to its workspace (normal). Autonomous invocation is allowed by default but not uniquely granted here. Spawning sub-agents is a runtime behavior, not a manifest persistence/privilege escalation.
assessment: This skill looks functionally coherent for generating multi-person investment reports, but you should verify two things before installing or running it: (1) How 'send to current Discord channel' is implemented — ask the author whether a Discord webhook/token is required and where credentials would be stored; do not provide broad Discord tokens unless you trust the skill. If the agent uses your existing Discord integration, be aware it will post potentially sensitive portfolio or query text to that channel. (2) The skill spawns five parallel sub-agents and uses web_search; confirm what context (conversation history, files, or secrets) those sub-agents can access so you don't accidentally leak sensitive data. Everything else (local reference files, price fetch from stooq.com, saving to workspace) is expected and proportionate. If you want to reduce risk: run the skill in a sandboxed agent with no Discord/network posting permission, inspect fetch_price.py locally (it only queries stooq.com), and consider removing or disabling the automatic 'send to Discord' step until you verify the integration.

sections.Assessment: This skill looks functionally coherent for generating multi-person investment reports, but you should verify two things before installing or running it: (1) How 'send to current Discord channel' is implemented — ask the author whether a Discord webhook/token is required and where credentials would be stored; do not provide broad Discord tokens unless you trust the skill. purpose_capability: The name/description claim to read local (including protected) Office and text files. The shipped Python script reads text files, extracts XML from .docx and .xlsx ZIP packages, and uses only standard library modules—this matches the stated primary purpose. There are no unrelated environment variables, binaries, or services requested. The only mismatch: the description suggests handling 'encrypted/受保护' files accessible to authorized apps; the code does not implement decryption or password-unlocking of password-protected Office files and will fail on truly encrypted Office packages.
instruction_scope: SKILL.md instructs running the included Python script on a local path, and provides examples; it does not instruct reading unrelated system files or exfiltrating data. Note: the documentation claims '自动处理特殊编码' (automatic handling of special encodings), but the implementation always decodes text files as UTF-8 only, so it may error on files in other encodings.
install_mechanism: No install spec or remote downloads; the skill is instruction-only with a local Python script and standard-library dependencies. No network fetch or archive extraction during install—low installer risk.
credentials: The skill requests no environment variables, no credentials, and no config paths. The code uses only local file I/O and standard libraries—requested access is proportional to the stated purpose.
persistence_privilege: always is false and the skill does not request elevated or persistent platform privileges. Autonomous invocation is allowed by default (disable-model-invocation is false) but that is normal; there is no evidence the skill modifies other skills or agent-wide settings.
assessment: This skill appears to be a simple local reader that extracts text from plain files and Office .docx/.xlsx packages; it does not contact external endpoints or request secrets. Before installing or using it: (1) be aware it does NOT bypass password protection or decrypt truly encrypted Office files—password-protected/encrypted .docx/.xlsx will likely fail; (2) its text reader always decodes as UTF-8 despite claims of automatic encoding detection—files in other encodings may error; (3) because it reads arbitrary local files, avoid running it against highly sensitive data unless you trust the environment and have inspected the code (which is short and uses only standard libs); (4) if you need decryption of protected files, look for explicit support for Office encryption/passwords or official APIs rather than relying on this tool. If you want higher assurance, verify the referenced GitHub repository and run the script in a controlled environment with non-sensitive test files first.

purpose_capability: Name/description (SEO research, outline, draft, scoring) align with declared requirements: python3 and curl are reasonable for fetching pages and running small scripts. The included title_generator.py is a benign utility that fits the stated features.

persistence_privilege: Skill does not request always:true, does not modify other skills or system-wide settings, and is user-invocable. Autonomous invocation is allowed by platform default but is not combined here with broad privileges.
assessment: This skill appears coherent and low-risk: it needs python3 and curl, includes a harmless title-generation script, and relies on a SearXNG instance for research. Before installing: (1) be cautious where SEARXNG_URL points — prefer a local or trusted instance to avoid leaking search queries or pasted article content; (2) avoid pasting sensitive/private articles into the skill when using a remote research endpoint; (3) review the included scripts (title_generator.py is small and benign) and monitor network activity on first use; (4) note the author/homepage are missing, so trust is based on the package contents rather than an established publisher. If you need stronger assurance, ask the publisher for a homepage or audit logs, or run the skill in an isolated environment first.

purpose_capability: Name/description align with behavior: the script calls a wardrobe API, downloads images and composes them with ImageMagick. Requiring an API key (AICLOSET_API_KEY) and ImageMagick is coherent for this purpose. Minor mismatch: SKILL.md instructs adding a key to ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json but the registry metadata lists no required config paths.
instruction_scope: SKILL.md mandates executing scripts/generate_outfit.py and that the agent read openclaw.json (if env var absent). The script reads a local config file (~/.openclaw/openclaw.json), downloads arbitrary image URLs returned by the remote API, runs external binaries (magick, open/xdg-open), and opens the generated image locally. Reading the user's config file and executing platform commands are within the skill's functional scope, but the SKILL.md's insistence on always running the provided script and the undocumented dependency on the openclaw.json path broaden the agent's privileges and should be made explicit in metadata.
install_mechanism: No install spec — instruction-only with a bundled Python script. No external downloads/installers are fetched by the skill itself. The script uses only the Python standard library and calls ImageMagick (external binary), which is documented in SKILL.md.
credentials: The only declared primary credential is AICLOSET_API_KEY, which is appropriate. However, the skill implicitly reads ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json for a stored apiKey if the env var is missing; the registry metadata does not list required config paths. Also the API key will be sent to a third-party domain (https://aicloset-dev-h5.wxbjq.top), which appears to be a development/third-party host — you should verify the trustworthiness and scope of any API key you provide.
persistence_privilege: The skill does not request always: true and does not modify other skills or system settings. It writes temporary files under the system temp directory, which is expected for image composition. It does open the resulting image with platform viewer commands.
assessment: This skill is functionally coherent with a wardrobe/outfit generator, but review before installing: - Verify the API host (https://aicloset-dev-h5.wxbjq.top). Only give an API key if you trust that service; prefer an API key with limited scope/ability to revoke it if concerned. - The script will read ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json if no AICLOSET_API_KEY env var is set. The registry metadata did not declare that config path — consider whether you are comfortable storing a key there. - The script downloads images from URLs returned by the API and runs ImageMagick and your OS image viewer (magick/open/xdg-open). These operations are expected for image composition but do execute external binaries and open local files; avoid running with elevated privileges. - If you want to reduce risk, create a dedicated API key with minimal permissions and test the skill in an isolated environment first. If you need higher confidence, ask the publisher for: (1) a clear, official API hostname (not a dev domain), (2) explicit declaration of required config paths in the registry metadata, and (3) an audit or signed provenance for the skill source.

purpose_capability: Name/description match the code and dependencies: the package depends on futu-api and pydantic and implements account, quote, and trade modules that call the Futu OpenAPI. Required binaries (python3, pip) are appropriate for the stated purpose.
instruction_scope: Runtime instructions ask the user to install the package, create json/config.json with Futu credentials, and use the provided wrapper functions. The skill writes account query results to json/account_info.json and may read/write json/config.json; this is expected for a trading client but is sensitive and worth noting.
install_mechanism: No remote download URLs or obscure installers detected. SKILL.md directs a local 'pip install -e .' (source contains pyproject.toml and requirements.txt) which is standard for local Python packages. There is no install action that fetches code from untrusted hosts within the skill metadata.
credentials: The skill requests no environment variables and uses a local JSON config file for credentials (trade_password or trade_password_md5). Storing plaintext passwords or MD5 in json/config.json is functional but sensitive — the skill does not declare env-based credential usage, so users must protect the config file manually.
persistence_privilege: always is false and model invocation is allowed (platform default). The skill does not try to modify other skills or system-wide agent settings. Be aware: because it can execute trades, an agent invoking it autonomously could perform real trades if configured for REAL.
assessment: This skill appears to implement exactly what it claims: a Futu/OpenD trading wrapper. Before installing or handing it to an agent, consider the following: - Treat json/config.json as highly sensitive: do not store real trading passwords in source-control, set strict file permissions (chmod 600), or prefer providing the password at runtime rather than keeping plaintext in the file. - Default to SIMULATE for testing (the config default and code support 'SIMULATE'); test all workflows with a simulation account before using REAL. - The skill writes account_info.json locally on each account query — ensure that file's location is acceptable for your threat model (it contains account metadata) and is protected. - Review the code yourself (or in a sandbox) if you will run pip install -e .; installing in editable mode will register the package locally and execute package metadata build steps. - If you allow autonomous agent invocation, restrict the agent's permissions or confirm prompts for REAL trades to avoid unintended live orders. If you are comfortable with these trade-offs and follow the recommendation to test in SIMULATE and protect the config file, the skill is internally consistent with its stated purpose.

Storing plaintext passwords or MD5 in json/config.json is functional but sensitive — the skill does not declare env-based credential usage, so users must protect the config file manually.
sections.Persistence & Privilege: always is false and model invocation is allowed (platform default). The skill does not try to modify other skills or system-wide agent settings. Be aware: because it can execute trades, an agent invoking it autonomously could perform real trades if configured for REAL.
sections.Assessment: This skill appears to implement exactly what it claims: a Futu/OpenD trading wrapper. Before installing or handing it to an agent, consider the following: - Treat json/config.json as highly sensitive: do not store real trading passwords in source-control, set strict file permissions (chmod 600), or prefer providing the password at runtime rather than keeping plaintext in the file. - Default to SIMULATE for testing (the config default and code support 'SIMULATE'); test all workflows with a simulation account before using REAL. - The skill writes account_info.json locally on each account query — ensure that file's location is acceptable for your threat model (it contains account metadata) and is protected. - Review the code yourself (or in a sandbox) if you will run pip install -e .; installing in editable mode will register the package locally and execute package metadata build steps. - If you allow autonomous agent invocation, restrict the agent's permissions or confirm prompts for REAL trades to avoid unintended live orders. If you are comfortable with these trade-offs and follow the recommendation to test in SIMULATE and protect the config file, the skill is internally consistent with its stated purpose.

purpose_capability: The SKILL.md, README and included HTML demos consistently implement a zero-dependency HTML slide generator with optional PPTX export. One small inconsistency: the top-level Name field in the provided metadata is 'Genppt' while the skill's files, SKILL.md and repository references use 'slide-creator' / html-slide-creator — likely a packaging/label mismatch but not fatal to the feature alignment.
instruction_scope: Runtime instructions explicitly read local files (resources/, PLANNING.md, existing .html/.pptx), generate/overwrite presentation HTML, and run a Python export script. These behaviors are appropriate for a presentation tool, but the skill will 'silently scan' a resources/ folder and save PLANNING.md / presentation files without additional prompts — a privacy/consent consideration rather than an incoherence.
install_mechanism: Dependencies are standard Python packages (Pillow, python-pptx, playwright) installed via the declared package mechanism. This is proportional to the stated PPT export and image pipeline. One operational caveat: Playwright can trigger browser downloads or require additional setup; the SKILL.md claims it will use the user's existing system Chrome (no Chromium download), which is plausible but may require additional Playwright configuration or fail if Playwright's install hooks attempt to fetch browsers.
credentials: The skill requests no environment variables, no credentials, and no config paths. Its file-read/write behavior is limited to the working directory (resources/, PLANNING.md, generated HTML, exported PPTX), which is proportionate to its purpose.
persistence_privilege: The skill is not marked always:true and uses normal agent invocation. It writes files into the working directory (creating PLANNING.md, presentation.html, saving inline edits, and export outputs) which is expected for this functionality. There is no evidence it changes other skills or system-wide agent settings.
assessment: This skill appears to do what it says: generate single-file HTML presentations and optionally export PPTX via a Python script. Before installing, consider: 1) Confirm the skill source (metadata shows a GitHub homepage which matches the repository files — but the listed skill name 'Genppt' vs internal name 'slide-creator' is inconsistent; verify you are installing the intended package). 2) Review scripts/scripts/export-pptx.py and scripts/screenshot-demos.py yourself (they will run locally) to be comfortable with what they read/write. 3) The skill will scan a local resources/ folder silently and will write PLANNING.md, presentation.html, and exported .pptx files — back up any important files in the target directory. 4) Installing 'playwright' may require extra steps or may attempt to download browser binaries unless configured to reuse system Chrome; be prepared to run/permit any Playwright setup instructions or run the export in an isolated environment. 5) If you need stronger isolation, run the export and installs inside a controlled virtualenv/container. The runtime instructions and code perform network calls only to the stated data sources (barttorvik.com, ESPN public picks URL, and optional KenPom). The code reads/writes cache files under a local data/ directory and does not instruct reading unrelated system files or environment variables beyond the optional KenPom creds.
install_mechanism: No install spec is provided (instruction-only at registry level), and the README lists minimal Python dependencies (requests, optional kenpompy). No downloaded archives, URL shorteners, or remote installers are used by the bundle itself.
credentials: No required environment variables are declared; only optional KENPOM_EMAIL and KENPOM_PASSWORD are referenced for the KenPom pathway which legitimately requires credentials. No other SECRET/TOKEN/PASSWORD variables are requested.
persistence_privilege: Skill does not request always:true and does not modify other skills. It creates a local data/ cache directory for fetched data (expected behavior) but does not escalate privileges or alter system-wide agent settings.
assessment: This skill appears coherent and implements what it claims: it will make outgoing HTTP requests to Bart Torvik, ESPN (and optionally KenPom), and it caches JSON under a local data/ folder. If you plan to use KenPom features, provide only a dedicated KenPom credential and be aware those credentials are used for logging into that service. If you have concerns about network access or local caching, run the skill in an isolated environment (virtualenv/container) or review the included source files (they are small and readable). Finally, the SKILL.md references a GitHub repo for agent competitions — check that external repo if you intend to submit brackets.

purpose_capability: Name, description, SKILL.md and code all align: the skill implements Tuya Cloud list/read/control operations and legitimately needs Tuya API credentials and region endpoint. However, registry metadata declares no required environment variables or primary credential while the SKILL.md and code explicitly require TUYA_ACCESS_ID and TUYA_ACCESS_SECRET (and optionally TUYA_API_ENDPOINT). This metadata mismatch is an inconsistency.
instruction_scope: The runtime instructions and the Python code operate only on Tuya Cloud APIs and local .env/test_data.env files. There are no instructions to read unrelated system files or transmit data to endpoints other than Tuya's OpenAPI endpoints described in the SKILL.md.
install_mechanism: This is an instruction-only skill with a simple requirements.txt (tinytuya, python-dotenv). There is no arbitrary URL download or extract step; dependencies come from PyPI, which is expected for this purpose.
credentials: The requested secrets (TUYA_ACCESS_ID and TUYA_ACCESS_SECRET) are appropriate for controlling Tuya devices. The concern is that the skill/README/SKILL.md require these env vars but the registry metadata did not declare them, which can mislead users or automated permission gating. Tests and examples also expect private device IDs in a test_data.env file (SENSOR_DEVICE_ID, WATER_VALVE_DEVICE_ID, etc.). The code uses dotenv.load_dotenv(override=True), which will load/override environment variables from a local .env file—be cautious about storing secrets in plaintext in project directories.
persistence_privilege: The skill is not always-enabled and does not request system-wide persistence or modification of other skill configs. Autonomous invocation is permitted (platform default) but the skill does not request elevated platform privileges.
assessment: This skill appears to implement exactly what it says (listing, reading, and controlling Tuya Cloud devices) and legitimately needs your Tuya API credentials and device IDs. Before installing or running it: - Expect to supply TUYA_ACCESS_ID and TUYA_ACCESS_SECRET (and optionally TUYA_API_ENDPOINT) via a .env file or environment variables — the registry metadata omitted these, so double-check permission prompts. - Do not store production credentials in a shared repo or a world-readable .env; run the skill in an isolated environment and consider using a short-lived/limited API key with only the permissions needed. - Tests require device IDs (SENSOR_DEVICE_ID, WATER_VALVE_DEVICE_ID, etc.) in tests/test_data.env — avoid committing those IDs or real credentials. - Review scripts/config.py for any hard-coded device IDs you don't control; remove or replace them if they expose private device identifiers. - If you need higher assurance, request the publisher update registry metadata to declare required env vars and provide a trusted homepage/source, or review the tinytuya library versions being installed. Given the metadata mismatch, proceed cautiously; the code itself is coherent but the packaging/metadata failure reduces trust.

purpose_capability: Name/description, required binaries (python3), and requested env vars (BAIDU_API_KEY, BAIDU_SECRET_KEY) match an OCR integration. However the shipped Python script embeds a different API_KEY/SECRET_KEY pair and does not read environment variables — the credential requirements in metadata/SKILL.md are thus inconsistent with the actual code.
instruction_scope: SKILL.md instructs the agent/user to set BAIDU_API_KEY and BAIDU_SECRET_KEY and to run the bundled script. The script, however, ignores env/config and uses hard-coded credentials to call Baidu's OCR endpoints, meaning images provided to the skill will be uploaded under the included account rather than the user's. Instructions otherwise stay within OCR purpose and use legitimate Baidu endpoints.
install_mechanism: No external install/downloads or third-party URLs — the skill is instruction-only with a local Python script. This low install footprint reduces supply-chain risk. Metadata lists dependencies (requests, Pillow); the script imports requests but not Pillow (minor inconsistency).
credentials: The skill declares BAIDU_API_KEY and BAIDU_SECRET_KEY as required—which is appropriate for an OCR integration—but the code bypasses these and uses embedded credentials. That is disproportionate and suspicious because it removes the need for the user's keys and routes data through the publisher's account.
persistence_privilege: No 'always: true', no install-time changes to system or other skills, and the skill does not request elevated system privileges. It only requires running a Python script on demand.
assessment: Do not run this skill as-is on sensitive images. The included baidu_ocr.py contains hard-coded BAIDU API credentials (visible in the file and referenced in the activation guide), while SKILL.md tells you to set your own credentials — this mismatch means your images will likely be uploaded using the publisher's Baidu account (potential privacy, quota, and billing concerns). Before installing or running: 1) Inspect baidu_ocr.py and remove the embedded API_KEY/SECRET_KEY or replace them with code that reads BAIDU_API_KEY and BAIDU_SECRET_KEY from the environment/config. 2) Confirm the code prioritizes environment variables over any hard-coded defaults. 3) Optionally, run the script in an isolated environment or sandbox and monitor network calls to verify destination endpoints. 4) If you cannot or will not modify the code, consider rejecting the skill or asking the publisher why their credentials are embedded and whether they intend to collect images. If you previously used the embedded key unintentionally, consider contacting Baidu or rotating/revoking keys associated with your own account and review any sensitive data that may have been sent. Additional info that would change this assessment: if the maintainer publishes an updated version that removes hard-coded credentials and clearly documents that only the user's API keys are used, the concern would be resolved.

purpose_capability: The name/description (skill security scanner) match the main scanner code (scripts/skillshield.py). However the package also contains Moltbook integration and an upload script which go beyond a pure local scanner; those integrations are plausible but not strictly required for the stated purpose. The manifest/metadata declared no required env vars or credentials, yet code expects a Moltbook API key. Also scripts/publish.py expects a _meta.json file that is not present in the manifest — another mismatch.
instruction_scope: SKILL.md instructs running local scanner scripts and a 'guardian' daemon to monitor Moltbook. The code implements monitoring and an uploader that will POST content to an external Moltbook service. While scanning local skill files is expected, the included upload_to_moltbook.py will publish content to an external service (and includes a default API token). Running the guardian or upload script would transmit data externally; the README and scripts also reference absolute internal paths which may expose local information if posted.
install_mechanism: There is no install spec (instruction-only), so nothing is automatically downloaded or installed by the platform. The security surface is the included Python scripts. That reduces supply-chain install risk, but you still must inspect or sandbox those scripts before running them.
credentials: Skill metadata declares no required environment variables or credentials, yet multiple scripts read MOLTBOOK_API_KEY from the environment. More importantly, upload_to_moltbook.py contains a hard-coded API key fallback ('moltbook_sk_JMb6t_WI-xq7SQapbAYXF9BFBPuXBuuM'), which is a secret embedded in the code — disproportionate and potentially exploitable. The presence of an embedded token that will be used to post to an external service is the main proportionality issue.
persistence_privilege: The skill is not marked always:true and does not request persistent system-wide privileges. The guardian supports a long-running monitor (user-invoked). Autonomous invocation of skills is enabled by default on the platform (not flagged here), but combined with the hard-coded API token and external posting, a running guardian could have a wider blast radius if started.
assessment: This package appears to implement a legitimate local skill scanner, but it contains surprising external-integration code you should not run blindly. Key points to consider before installing or running: 1) upload_to_moltbook.py has a hard-coded Moltbook API token — treat that as sensitive and potentially active; do not run the uploader unless you trust the token and endpoint. 2) The guardian/monitor will query and (intended) post to an external service — run only in a sandbox and inspect network calls first. 3) The skill metadata declares no required env vars but the code uses MOLTBOOK_API_KEY; confirm what keys will actually be used and rotate/revoke any embedded tokens. 4) Review scripts for any code paths that read or post local filesystem information (the uploader's post content references local paths). 5) Prefer running the scanner with read-only access to a copy of the skill repository, or inspect/grep the code first (search for 'MOLTBOOK', 'API_KEY', 'create_post', 'urlopen', 'urllib.request'). If you need the scanner but not the network behavior, remove or disable the Moltbook-related scripts before use.

sections.Instruction Scope: SKILL.md instructs running local scanner scripts and a 'guardian' daemon to monitor Moltbook. The code implements monitoring and an uploader that will POST content to an external Moltbook service. While scanning local skill files is expected, the included upload_to_moltbook.py will publish content to an external service (and includes a default API token). Running the guardian or upload script would transmit data externally; the README and scripts also reference absolute internal paths which may expose local information if posted.
sections.Install Mechanism: There is no install spec (instruction-only), so nothing is automatically downloaded or installed by the platform. The security surface is the included Python scripts. That reduces supply-chain install risk, but you still must inspect or sandbox those scripts before running them. ! CREDENTIALS Skill metadata declares no required environment variables or credentials, yet multiple scripts read MOLTBOOK_API_KEY from the environment. More importantly, upload_to_moltbook.py contains a hard-coded API key fallback ('moltbook_sk_JMb6t_WI-xq7SQapbAYXF9BFBPuXBuuM'), which is a secret embedded in the code — disproportionate and potentially exploitable. The presence of an embedded token that will be used to post to an external service is the main proportionality issue.
sections.Persistence & Privilege: The skill is not marked always:true and does not request persistent system-wide privileges. The guardian supports a long-running monitor (user-invoked). Autonomous invocation of skills is enabled by default on the platform (not flagged here), but combined with the hard-coded API token and external posting, a running guardian could have a wider blast radius if started.
sections.Assessment: This package appears to implement a legitimate local skill scanner, but it contains surprising external-integration code you should not run blindly. Key points to consider before installing or running: 1) upload_to_moltbook.py has a hard-coded Moltbook API token — treat that as sensitive and potentially active; do not run the uploader unless you trust the token and endpoint. 2) The guardian/monitor will query and (intended) post to an external service — run only in a sandbox and inspect network calls first. 3) The skill metadata declares no required env vars but the code uses MOLTBOOK_API_KEY; confirm what keys will actually be used and rotate/revoke any embedded tokens. 4) Review scripts for any code paths that read or post local filesystem information (the uploader's post content references local paths). 5) Prefer running the scanner with read-only access to a copy of the skill repository, or inspect/grep the code first (search for 'MOLTBOOK', 'API_KEY', 'create_post', 'urlopen', 'urllib.request'). If you need the scanner but not the network behavior, remove or disable the Moltbook-related scripts before use.

purpose_capability: Name/description claim image generation using Volcano Engine Seedream 5.0; the package only requires a CLI 'uv' runtime and a Volcano API key, and the included script calls the documented Volcano API endpoint. Required items are proportional to the stated purpose.
instruction_scope: SKILL.md instructs running the provided script via 'uv' with prompts, input-image paths or URLs, and an API key. The script uploads input images (base64 or URL) and writes generated images to disk—behaviour matches the skill purpose. It only reads API key env variables and local input images; nothing in the instructions asks for unrelated system data.
install_mechanism: Install specifies a single brew formula 'uv' (a known tool for running Python scripts). No downloads from untrusted URLs or archive extraction are present. Script dependencies (requests, pillow) are declared in-file; this is reasonable for the functionality.
credentials: Only VOLC_API_KEY (primary) or ARK_API_KEY is required, which is exactly the credential needed to call Volcano Engine APIs. No unrelated secrets, config paths, or additional credentials are requested.
persistence_privilege: always is false and the skill does not request persistent elevated privileges or modify other skills. It documents configuring keys via openclaw.json or UI, which is normal for credential injection.
assessment: This skill appears to do what it says: it will send prompts and any provided input images to Volcano Engine's image generation API and save returned images locally. Before installing: 1) Only provide an API key you trust and are willing to use with a third‑party image service (consider a scoped/ephemeral key if available). 2) Do not upload sensitive images (IDs, private photos) because input images are transmitted to the remote API. 3) Verify the 'uv' brew package source on your platform if you care about install provenance. 4) If you use shared or persisted openclaw.json credentials, be aware they are stored locally in that config file and will be injected as VOLC_API_KEY. 5) Review the included script if you need stricter controls (e.g., preventing upload of local files or restricting web-search).

purpose_capability: The name/description (skill creation, eval, benchmarking, description optimization) matches the included scripts and agent guides: many files are evaluation/packaging utilities (run_eval.py, run_loop.py, generate_review.py, grader/comparator/analyzer agent prompts). It is reasonable that a skill-creator needs to read skill files, transcripts, and outputs. However, the included tools traverse and embed arbitrary files from a provided workspace and can include binary files (PDF/XLSX) as base64, which is broader access than a casual 'skill authoring helper' might imply.
instruction_scope: SKILL.md and the agent files explicitly instruct reading skill SKILL.md files, transcripts, outputs, and running evaluation scripts. generate_review.py recursively finds runs under a workspace, reads many file types, base64-embeds binaries, and prepares a standalone HTML review. These instructions allow reading and packaging any file under the workspace directory (and some parent checks), so if the workspace contains secrets or unrelated data those could be included in the generated review and sent externally. The SKILL.md also mentions calling 'claude -p' and using sessions_spawn; invoking an external CLI or subagent increases scope and network interaction if present.
install_mechanism: No install spec — instruction- and script-only. That's lower risk than arbitrary downloads. The Python scripts use only stdlib functionality (as advertised) so there is no hidden remote install. Still, scripts call subprocesses (e.g., lsof) when available.
credentials: The skill declares no required environment variables or credentials, which aligns with its stated purpose. There are no declared external tokens or keys requested. That said, the code can call external CLIs (e.g., 'claude -p' if installed) and will attempt to open the network (viewer HTML references Google Fonts) and run local commands (lsof), so the absence of declared creds is coherent but doesn't eliminate runtime network/local interactions.
persistence_privilege: always: false and default model invocation are preserved (no forced always-on). The skill does not request to modify other skills or global agent settings. Scripts write or embed review/feedback files within the supplied workspace, which is expected for an eval tool and scoped to the workspace rather than global config.
assessment: This skill is mostly coherent with its purpose, but exercise caution before running it against a workspace that may contain secrets or sensitive files. Specific points to consider: - The included scripts (e.g., eval-viewer/generate_review.py) recursively read files from a workspace and embed text/binary files (PDF, XLSX, images) into a standalone HTML, so any secrets in that workspace could end up embedded in the generated HTML. - The scripts use subprocess.run (e.g., to call lsof to kill a port) and may attempt to invoke an external CLI ('claude -p') if present — review those calls and avoid running them if you don't trust the environment or the external tools. - The generated viewer.html loads Google Fonts (network call) and the Python script may spawn a tiny HTTP server and open a browser; if you plan to share the generated HTML, confirm it contains only intended content. Mitigations before installing/using: - Inspect the repository locally (you already have the files) and read generate_review.py and run_loop.py to understand exactly which paths they read and which subprocesses they call. - Run the scripts only on a dedicated, scrubbed workspace that contains only the eval artifacts you intend to expose. Remove any credentials, private keys, or unrelated files first. - If you need extra safety, run the scripts in an isolated environment (container, VM) with no network and limited filesystem mounts. - If you won't use the optional external CLI (claude -p), disable or remove those code paths to avoid accidental invocation. - Prefer manual review of the generated HTML before sending it to others; verify that the content included is expected. Given these behaviors, the skill is not clearly malicious but has broader file- and process-access than casual users might expect — classify as suspicious until you confirm you're running it in a safe, intended workspace.

purpose_capability: The name/description (convert X/Twitter content to Markdown using a reverse-engineered API) matches the code: the skill fetches X GraphQL endpoints and converts tweets/articles to Markdown. Using cookies or a bearer token to access private/expanded content is plausible for this purpose. However, the SKILL.md declares no required environment variables while the code reads multiple X-specific env vars (X_AUTH_TOKEN, X_CT0, X_GUEST_TOKEN, X_TWID, X_BEARER_TOKEN, X_USER_AGENT, X_CLIENT_TRANSACTION_ID, X_CHROME_PATH, etc.). That mismatch between declared requirements and actual behavior is noteworthy.
instruction_scope: Runtime instructions and code will: check/create consent and EXTEND.md preference files in the user's home/project directories; read/write a cookie file; attempt to load cookies from environment variables; and, if needed, launch/attach to a local Chrome instance via the Chrome DevTools Protocol to capture cookies. Those actions go beyond simple HTTP requests to X and access local browser state and files. SKILL.md does require user consent and an explicit first-time setup dialog, but it does not fully document the environment variables or the full extent of local browser/profile access the scripts may use.
install_mechanism: There is no remote install/download step; scripts are included in the bundle and executed via bun (or npx -y bun). No external arbitrary URL downloads or installers are used. The injected vendor package (baoyu-chrome-cdp) is bundled in the source tree rather than pulled remotely, which reduces one class of supply-chain risk but still executes local code when run.
credentials: Although the skill declares no required environment variables, the code accepts and uses many potentially sensitive env vars (X_AUTH_TOKEN, X_CT0, X_GUEST_TOKEN, X_TWID, X_BEARER_TOKEN, X_USER_AGENT, X_CLIENT_TRANSACTION_ID, X_CHROME_PATH, X_DEBUG_PORT). It will also write cookie files and may log truncated auth_token/ct0 values. Requesting or reading these secrets is plausible for a reverse-engineered X client, but the lack of explicit declaration/documentation and the presence of operations that read local browser cookies means the privilege requested is sensitive and should be considered carefully.
persistence_privilege: The skill does not force permanent inclusion (always: false). It will create and write consent.json, EXTEND.md, and a cookie file under user/project config paths; it may also create directories and save downloaded media. It will not (from the visible code) modify other skills or system-wide agent settings, but it does interact with the user's browser profile and may launch Chrome (or attach to an existing Chrome debug port).
assessment: This skill legitimately needs cookies or tokens to access private or fuller X content, but it obtains them by reading environment variables, writing/reading cookie files, and optionally launching/attaching to your local Chrome to extract cookies. Before installing or running it: - Only use it if you trust the author/source (review the included scripts yourself if possible). - Expect it to create files under your home directory (consent.json, EXTEND.md, cookie file) and possibly save media locally. - Do not provide long-lived production account credentials unless you accept the risk; consider using a throwaway X account if you must log in. - If you are uncomfortable with a tool that reads browser cookies or requires launching Chrome, do not install/run it. - If you proceed, review the code paths that read environment variables and write cookie files, and consider running the tool in an isolated environment (container or VM) to reduce exposure. - The mismatch between SKILL.md (no env vars declared) and the actual code reading many X-related env vars is a red flag: ask the publisher to clarify what secrets the skill may read and why. purpose_capability: Name/description match the code and runtime instructions: scripts implement regular posts and headline articles via Chrome CDP, markdown-to-HTML conversion, clipboard helpers, and paste helpers. Required binaries (bun or npx) are reasonable for executing the provided TypeScript scripts.
instruction_scope: SKILL.md instructs the agent to run local scripts from the scripts/ folder and to reuse or open Chrome profiles and CDP ports. The instructions explicitly suggest killing existing Chrome CDP instances automatically when the debug port isn't ready and to operate on Chrome user-data dirs; these are within the goal of driving a real browser but are sensitive actions (terminating browser processes, activating apps, performing real paste keystrokes, reading/writing profile files).
install_mechanism: No external install spec; skill is instruction/script-based and ships vendored dependencies. Nothing is downloaded at runtime from obscure URLs. Execution relies on local runtimes/tools (bun/npx, osascript/xdotool/powershell/etc.) which is expected for this approach.
credentials: The registry metadata declares no required env vars, but the code honors optional env overrides (e.g., BAOYU_CHROME_PATH, BAOYU_SHARED_PROFILE) and probes standard Chrome user-data dirs under the user's home. That access is coherent with reusing an existing logged-in Chrome session, but it means the skill will read (and potentially reuse) browser profile data beyond what the manifest declares.
persistence_privilege: always:false and no install step that makes the skill persistently active. The skill may reuse or launch Chrome with a remote-debugging port and will operate on existing browser profiles, but it does not modify other skills or request system-wide privileges in its metadata.
assessment: This skill appears to be what it claims, but it performs sensitive local actions to bypass anti-bot defenses: it reads Chrome user-data directories, may start or reuse Chrome with a remote-debugging port, sends real paste keystrokes (osascript/xdotool/powershell), manipulates the system clipboard, and its troubleshooting guidance suggests killing existing Chrome/CDP processes automatically. Before installing/running: 1) Review the scripts (weibo-article.ts / weibo-post.ts) yourself; 2) Prefer running with a dedicated or disposable Chrome profile (use --profile) instead of your main profile to avoid leaking sessions or affecting other tabs; 3) Back up important Chrome profile data in case processes are killed or files are read; 4) Be aware the skill will spawn platform commands (swift, osascript, xdotool, powershell, etc.); 5) If you want extra safety, run the skill in an isolated environment (VM/container) or manually step through the commands rather than allowing full automation. These actions are within the skill's stated scope, but they do involve filesystem writes and interaction with a local browser process (see note about attaching to an existing Chrome instance).
sections.Install Mechanism: No install spec is provided (instruction-only), and the skill relies on bundled TypeScript scripts executed with bun/npx. That is low-risk compared with remote archive downloads. Dependencies are typical JS libs for HTML parsing/conversion and a vendored baoyu-chrome-cdp module.
sections.Credentials: The skill requests no secrets or external API keys. It does read/write configuration in ~/.baoyu-skills or project .baoyu-skills and writes output files to chosen directories. It may launch a new Chrome instance with a dedicated profile dir or attach to an existing Chrome debug port — attaching to an existing browser (if detected) could expose tabs, cookies, and other session state accessible via CDP, so users should be aware and prefer launching an isolated browser or set URL_CHROME_PATH if concerned.
sections.Persistence & Privilege: always:false and no platform-wide modifications are requested. The skill stores preferences in its own EXTEND.md locations and writes output files, which is expected behavior for this tool and not an elevated privilege.
sections.Assessment: This skill is coherent for saving webpages as markdown, but consider the following before installing/running: (1) It will create and edit EXTEND.md in a project or user config directory and will write markdown and captured HTML to your filesystem — confirm the output paths you select. (2) It can launch Chrome with a dedicated profile, or attach to an existing Chrome debug instance if one is found; attaching to an existing browser may expose cookies, localStorage, and open tabs to the skill via CDP — if you want to avoid that, ensure no Chrome instance is running with remote debugging or prefer allowing the skill to launch a new browser profile. (3) As a fallback it will fetch markdown from https://defuddle.md; network access to that endpoint is expected. (4) The skill runs TypeScript scripts via bun or npx; run it in an environment you control (or sandbox) if you have concerns. If you want higher assurance, review the vendored baoyu-chrome-cdp code and the scripts in this bundle (they are included) or run the conversion locally with an isolated browser profile.
